skill.name: nano-banana-edit
skill.description: Edit images with Google Nano Banana 2 (image-to-image edit endpoint) on RunComfy. Documents Nano Banana Edit's strengths (preserve subject identity, swap background, localize edits with spatial language, multi-image batch edits up to 20 inputs), the schema, and when to route to GPT Image 2 edit / Flux Kontext / Nano Banana 2 t2i instead. Calls `runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "nano banana edit", "edit with nano banana", "image edit nano banana", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Edit images with Google Nano Banana 2 image-to-image edit endpoint with batch support up to 20 inputs on RunComfy.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 78 potential issues, but evaluation reveals all are false positives. The skill is a thin wrapper for the RunComfy CLI that invokes `runcomfy run google/nano-banana-2/edit` to call a legitimate image editing API. The "external commands" detections are markdown bash command examples, not executable code. The "path traversal" flags reference placeholder parameter examples (`<absolute/path>`) in documentation. The "weak crypto" detection is frontmatter YAML. Network access is scoped to RunComfy API endpoints only. Security controls are properly documented including JSON string transmission (no shell injection) and secure token storage with mode 0600.

high_findings.title: Static Analysis Pattern Detected: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged lines 4 and 162 as containing weak crypto. Evaluation shows this is a false positive: line 4 is YAML frontmatter with model documentation, line 162 is exit code documentation. No cryptographic operations present in this skill.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Static scanner detected pattern in YAML frontmatter and exit code table that triggered crypto detection heuristic, but neither contains cryptographic operations.

high_findings.title: Static Analysis Pattern Detected: Path Traversal
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged lines 71, 82, 95 as path traversal. Evaluation shows this is a false positive: these lines contain `<absolute/value>` style placeholder examples in documentation explaining CLI parameter types. These are not actual path traversal attempts.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner flagged `<absolute/path>` parameter examples as traversal, but these are documented CLI parameter type examples with placeholder names, not actual code execution.

high_findings.title: Static Analysis Pattern Detected: Hidden File Access
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged line 181 as hidden file access. Evaluation shows this is documented security practice: token is stored at `~/.config/runcomfy/token.json` with mode 0600 (owner-only read/write). This is intentional secure credential handling, not a vulnerability.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 181 explicitly documents secure token storage with appropriate file permissions. This is a security best practice, not an exploitation of hidden files.

medium_findings.title: Static Analysis Pattern Detected: External Commands
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged 57 locations as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. Evaluation shows this is a false positive: these are bash command examples in markdown documentation blocks showing CLI usage syntax. The skill is a documentation wrapper, not executable code.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations are markdown code fences containing `runcomfy run` command examples for documentation purposes. No backtick code execution occurs.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis Pattern Detected: Hardcoded URLs
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged multiple URLs as hardcoded. Evaluation shows these are legitimate API endpoints for the RunComfy Model API service being wrapped by this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs are legitimate RunComfy service endpoints documented for proper API usage. Network access is scoped to these whitelisted domains only.

low_findings.title: Static Analysis Pattern Detected: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged line 38 as system reconnaissance. Evaluation shows this is simple user intent detection for routing requests to the correct skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 38 contains routing logic for skill selection based on explicit user phrase matching ('nano banana edit'). Standard skill triggering pattern, not reconnaissance.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Google Nano Banana 2 on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Need to edit product photos, swap backgrounds, or batch process images? Nano Banana 2 Edit on RunComfy offers batch support for up to 20 images with strong subject identity preservation and spatial edit controls.
actual_capabilities: Edit images using Google Nano Banana 2 image-to-image model on RunComfy
Batch edit up to 20 images in a single request with consistent results
Preserve subject identity, pose, and clothing across edits
Localize edits with spatial language (background only, left object, upper-right corner)
Generate 1-4 output variations per edit request
Support multiple aspect ratios and resolutions from 0.5K to 4K
limitations: Requires valid RunComfy account and CLI login or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable
May drift with long compound prompts; split long edits into multiple passes
Multilingual in-image text edits are better handled by GPT Image 2 edit

use_cases.title: E-commerce Product Photography
use_cases.description: Batch edit product images with consistent backgrounds. Edit up to 20 SKU images in one call, preserving product identity while swapping studio backgrounds.

use_cases.title: Marketing Campaign Creative
use_cases.description: Create A/B variants for ad creative with seed-locked multiple outputs. Generate background swaps and localized edits for influencer and brand content.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing teams and social media managers

use_cases.title: Brand Asset Relocalization
use_cases.description: Adjust brand assets for different markets while preserving composition. Consistent text and palette swaps across localized content variants.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Background Swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the subject identity unchanged. Convert the background into [description of desired background].
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want to replace the entire background while preserving the main subject.

prompt_templates.title: Localized Object Edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the [specific object] only. Keep [list of elements to preserve], the [environment elements], and the lighting unchanged.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want to make targeted edits to specific areas without affecting other parts of the image.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Background Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: For each input image: keep the [subject identity/pose/clothing] unchanged. Convert the background to [description]. Center the subject at the same fraction of frame as the input.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when editing multiple product photos for consistent brand presentation.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Image Variation Set
prompt_templates.prompt: For each input: preserve the [identity/brand element]. Replace [specific element] with [new description]. Maintain consistent lighting and color palette across all outputs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use for generating A/B test variants or SKU gallery variations.

output_examples.input: User asks to swap background to a rainy neon cyberpunk street while keeping the subject identity
output_examples.output: The model generates 1-4 images with the same subject preserved against a rainy neon cyberpunk background, with consistent pose and framing.

output_examples.input: User asks to edit multiple product photos with a warm studio sweep background
output_examples.output: Batch of 3-5 product images with consistent warm grey studio backgrounds and subject positioning, ready for e-commerce listing.

best_practices: Lead with preservation instructions, end with the change. Always state what to keep first, then describe the edit.
Use concrete spatial language for localized edits: 'background only', 'left object', 'upper-right corner'.
Lock aspect_ratio and resolution for batch consistency. Use the same prompt grammar across batch edits.
anti_patterns: Avoid long compound instructions (change A and B and C) which cause model drift.
Do not use passive voice for edit instructions. Be imperative: 'Replace X with Y' not 'the background should be changed'.
Do not skip preservation goals. State explicitly what to keep unchanged or the model will subtly modify faces and brands.

faq.question: How many images can I edit in one batch request?
faq.answer: You can pass 1 to 20 input images per API call. The first image is treated as primary with the rest providing auxiliary cues.

faq.question: How many output images can I generate per request?
faq.answer: You can request 1 to 4 output images per call using the number_of_images parameter.

faq.question: How do I preserve subject identity during edits?
faq.answer: Lead your prompt with preservation instructions: 'Keep the subject identity, pose, and clothing unchanged.' Then state what to change.

faq.question: When should I use Nano Banana Edit vs other models?
faq.answer: Use Nano Banana Edit for batch edits, background swaps, and spatial localization. Use GPT Image 2 edit for multilingual text. Use Flux Kontext for single ref with precise local edits.

faq.question: What file formats are supported for output?
faq.answer: Output formats include PNG, JPEG, and WebP. Resolution options range from 0.5K to 4K.

faq.question: How does the skill handle API authentication?
faq.answer: Authentication uses runcomfy login for interactive sessions or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable for CI. Token is stored securely with mode 0600 permissions.
